{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27966","id":2731121766,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86iyZxm","number":27966,"title":"ENH: add a np.explain context manager to help teach new developers and debug ufunc loops","user":{"login":"mattip","id":823911,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgyMzkxMQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/823911?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip","html_url":"https://github.com/mattip","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mattip/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-12-10T20:27:57Z","updated_at":"2024-12-12T09:10:02Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

I have had this idea for some time now about a decorator that would trace calls into ufuncs and linalg, for instance
```
>>> with numpy.explain() as ex:
...    a + b
>>> print(ex)
called np.add -> LONG_addavx512 * 1024 -> LONG_add (not contiguous) *1024
```

The idea would be to provide some traceability into what NumPy is doing in a way that would help debug performance problems. The trick would be to make the mechanism fast and non-intrusive. I think something like a double linked list of
```
typedef _explainer {
    _explainer * prev;
    _explainer * next;
    const char * msg;
    int count;
} explainer;
```
and pass around the tail of the chain. The first call (in the decorator) would hold the head. Each annotated function would check `if not NULL` and either add a new node with a `const char` hard-coded `msg` or, if the `msg` matches, would increment the count. The context manager would pass in a first node, and would print and clean up memory. If no context manager is used, the NULL checks would be the only overhead.

Benefit:
- Developers could easily test that the code paths they are trying to hit are actually used. This is complicated today, since there is no direct visibility into what is being called.
- This could be used as a teaching tool both for "what is going on" new users and for "diving into NumPy" would-be contributors
- End users who hit a performance snag would be able to see why code is slow. Having the call count would show how non-contiguous data or buffering is harming performance. Having the inner loop name clearly available would show what dtype and loop optimization is actually used in practice.

Risks: 
- this may add too much overhead
- it would require finding a place to "tuck away" the pointer. For new-style ufuncs it would be easy since they have a `context` argument. There may be a way to hide the pointer in the unused `auxdata` arg to old-style ufuncs.
- cleanup under error conditions may be tricky and lead to memory leaks.
- the community may reject it

Next steps:
If this seems like something worth working on, it should hit the mailing list and also get a first working prototype. Then a NEP can be written to hammer out the finer details.

Alternatives:
- use python profilers that "know" about c-extensions like [py-spy](https://github.com/benfred/py-spy/blob/master/README.md) which already exists and can provide nice flame graphs.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2538282977","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27966#issuecomment-2538282977","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27966","id":2538282977,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86XSx_h","user":{"login":"rgommers","id":98330,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjk4MzMw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98330?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers","html_url":"https://github.com/rgommers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-12T09:10:01Z","updated_at":"2024-12-12T09:10:01Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Note Describe the issue:

The following code seems correct, and passes type checking under 2.1.3 but not 2.2.0:

```python
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(27446968)
n_states = 5

P = rng.random(size=(n_states, n_states))
p = rng.random(n_states)

p = P.T @ p
```

I suppose mypy is flagging that the float width can increase, but since float64 is used by random, that won't happen in this scenario.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
N/A
```


### Error message:

```shell
foo.py:10: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating[Any]]]", variable has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]]")  [assignment]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.13, mypy 1.13, numpy 2.2.0

_No response_","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27957/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":4,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27957/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2538384841","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27957#issuecomment-2538384841","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27957","id":2538384841,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86XTK3J","user":{"login":"rgommers","id":98330,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjk4MzMw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98330?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers","html_url":"https://github.com/rgommers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-12T09:44:15Z","updated_at":"2024-12-12T09:44:15Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I'll note that a number of the issues above are due to Mypy not being able to understand variables being reassigned. That is the case for both of these for example:
```python
P = rng.random(size=(n_states, n_states))
p = rng.random(n_states)
p = P.T @ p

x = np.zeros(1)
x = x / 1
```
Here the `p` and `x` variables' type should be inferred again when the type checker encounters a new `x =`/`p =`, but it doesn't . This is a very annoying and long-standing usability issue in Mypy. It also affects code patterns like this:
```python
def some_long_func():
    tmp = None
    # do something here that uses `tmp`, then further down use a fresh `tmp` variable:
    tmp = []
```
One fix that works in some cases, as @jorenham pointed out, is to explicitly type the variable with the looser of the two types (`p: npt.NDArray[np.floating]`), but the easier and more generic fix is just to rename the variable so it gets inferred correctly on first use.

Code patterns like that are common and there is nothing that can be done in NumPy to guard against new failures. For code like this:
```python
y = np.func1(...)
y = np.func2(...)
```
Mypy will choke as soon as `func1` gets more strict (and hence more accurate) type annotations and `func2` doesn't, if before their annotations were the same. Given that `func1` and `func2` can be any numpy functions, it follows that we cannot made any annotations more strict without causing these types of failures with Mypy. Pyright has no issues here.

> Is this a sign that the numerical computing community in Python is giving up on mypy as a standard type checker?

In addition to what @jorenham said in https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27957#issuecomment-2532461477: AFAIK this has not been discussed before, but I don't think it's an outrageous suggestion. Mypy is very problematic and beta quality at best - and that is probably not going to improve quickly given the weight of development history and backlog.

I also commented on the `array-api-strict` issue about a possible alternative solution (implement the buffer protocol there).

> Around our codebase that has been converted to support standard arrays, we still widely rely on `__array__`,

This may just be an issue of semantics, but we don't explicitly rely on `__array__` (almost?) anywhere. We use `np.asarray`, which tries `__array__` is a fallback method - but that's not quite the same thing. The actual thing we relied on can be more accurately phrased like this I think: "`np.asarray` works for the array types we are trying to support". 


> Outputs will typically be read-only after this step.

I don't think that is true. Could you explain why you think this is the case? Or is the claim more like "we should aim to treat the returned arrays as Blocked on https://github.com/data-apis/array-api-strict/pull/108.

Around our codebase that has been converted to support standard arrays, we still widely rely on `__array__`, but it is not in the standard. Therefore, array-api-strict would like to remove it. @mdhaber wrote this `xp_asarray` which delegates to `from_dlpack` under the hood for library-interchange.

Here I get all tests passing for `constants` and `cluster`.

[An important consideration](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21890#discussion_r1842815823) from @mdhaber in the PR this continues:

> Outputs will typically be read-only after this step. This seems like a big disadvantage compared to `xp.asarray` using the array protocol. I don't think we want to return read-only results from special functions. Is there a general solution that is efficient (e.g. not just `copy` the output before returning it) or do we have to be very careful about using `from_dlpack` only when necessary?

Perhaps we could attempt to use `__array__` and only fall-back to `from_dlpack` (and thus copying read-only outputs) if that fails. I'm looking at this for the first time - a few questions inline about intent and device support, and a couple of high-level comments here.

I also commented on the `array-api-strict` issue about a possible alternative solution (implement the buffer protocol there).

> Around our codebase that has been converted to support standard arrays, we still widely rely on `__array__`,

This may just be an issue of semantics, but we don't explicitly rely on `__array__` (almost?) anywhere. We use `np.asarray`, which tries `__array__` is a fallback method - but that's not quite the same thing. The actual thing we relied on can be more accurately phrased like this I think: "`np.asarray` works for the array types we are trying to support". 


> Outputs will typically be read-only after this step.

I don't think that is true. Could you explain why you think this is the case? Or is the claim more like "we should aim to treat the returned arrays as gh-21890. Blocked on https://github.com/data-apis/array-api-strict/pull/108.

Around our codebase that has been converted to support standard arrays, we still widely rely on `__array__`, but it is not in the standard. Therefore, array-api-strict would like to remove it. @mdhaber wrote this `xp_asarray` which delegates to `from_dlpack` under the hood for library-interchange.

Here I get all tests passing for `constants` and `cluster`.

[An important consideration](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21890#discussion_r1842815823) from @mdhaber in the PR this continues:

> Outputs will typically be read-only after this step. This seems like a big disadvantage compared to `xp.asarray` using the array protocol. I don't think we want to return read-only results from special functions. Is there a general solution that is efficient (e.g. not just `copy` the output before returning it) or do we have to be very careful about using `from_dlpack` only when necessary?

Perhaps we could attempt to use `__array__` and only fall-back to `from_dlpack` (and thus copying read-only outputs) if that fails. But I think that would still require an array-level mutability check such as that described in touched on this in a few places, in a [community meeting](https://hackmd.io/pyhudrC5TgSwdJtpPldHgQ#Agenda-for-July-24th-2024), on a PR I can't find back right now, and in in-person discussions. So here is a new issue with a concrete proposal._

We have experimental support for CuPy, PyTorch and JAX - all of which have CUDA (and in some cases ROCm and/or Intel XPUs) support, which we are not exercising in CI. As a result, we get issues like gh-21486 where someone finds locally that the test suite has regressed for a GPU-enabled array library. This is obviously not ideal, and we'd like a low-overhead and low-cost way to test PRs that a reviewer thinks may impact GPU support in CI.

Scikit-learn has such a CI job, see [the GHA workflow file for it](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/9d39f57399d6f1f7d8e8d4351dbc3e9244b98d28/.github/workflows/cuda-ci.yml) and @betatim's [blog post describing the design](https://betatim.github.io/posts/github-action-with-gpu/). 

The other thing we needed for it was a credit card from NumFOCUS that we can attach to this repo (with a limit on spending level, as described in the blog post) - we have that as well now. So we're all set in principle to add this.

Suggested logistics and design:
- The SciPy core team should sign off on the spending limit (just like we did for Cirrus CI usage). I'd suggest either $50/month or $100/month as the limit, and adjust if needed once we have some experience with it.
- The label-based triggering that scikit-learn has, where adding the label to a PR does a single run and then the label gets removed again so new commits don't auto-run the job again, seems nice - so let's copy it.
- We can start with building SciPy in the GPU-enabled CI runnner; if it becomes expensive we can move the SciPy wheel build to a separate (CPU-only, free) job, upload the wheel to the GHA cache, and then trigger a second job based on the first one to run the CUDA tests.
- The one change from the scikit-learn setup we should make is to use `pixi` rather than `conda-lock`. The latter makes sense for scikit-learn since they were already using it, but `pixi` will work better for us and we've mostly already got the setup worked out in https://github.com/rgommers/pixi-dev-scipystack. I don't want to propose adding that setup in the root of the repo, but only the single environment + lock file that we need to run this GPU job.

For the record, I reviewed https://docs.github.com/en/actions/security-for-github-actions/security-guides/security-hardening-for-github-actions#hardening-for-self-hosted-runners, and given my understanding that Cirrus Runners run on fresh instances for every new job and the limited permissions of the Cirrus Runners app, it's fine security-wise to use this self-hosted runner service on a public repo. There is also a note on this in the [Cirrus Runners docs here](https://cirrus-runners.app/setup/#runners-execution), that probably should be a bit more explicit about this given that GitHub's own docs try hard to warn people about self-hosted runners not being safe on public repos.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2541812711/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

============================================================= short test summary info =============================================================
FAILED scipy/ndimage/tests/test_interpolation.py::TestMapCoordinates::test_map_coordinates_large_data[cupy] - cupy_backends.cuda.api.runtime.CUDARuntimeError: cudaErrorInvalidValue: invalid argument
```

</details>

The Cirrus runner has a much better GPU than I do locally, so it's probably not a memory size issue. The test is a fairly ugly one, a regression test for a crash: 
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/18c4ebdfde513a97f86a08ba99f6530d4cfd7adb/scipy/ndimage/tests/test_interpolation.py#L599-L614

I then used the _Cancel workflow_ button in the Actions UI, and that didn't immediately seem to stop the job. After 5-10 minutes though it did end, however the logging data for the _Run CuPy tests_ which was visible during the test run itself then disappeared:

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/88f67a4d-15f3-4906-a846-45873dccb8c6)

So it'll be hard to diagnose a failure like that on CI.

Ending on some more good news:
- Performance is excellent: job startup, caching and build time are all 🚀 
- The GPU environment has >3 GB of packages for a single environment, however the 10 GB runner cache is happy (save/restore in ~45 seconds, no hiccups so far)

I'm still thinking about the best job configuration. It can all be done in a single job, but CuPy at least is quite slow. So splitting in multiple jobs may be the way to go (allows easier skips/changes), even if they don't run concurrently. The extra overhead of that is ~2 minutes:

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/623ef9e3-dce1-4a16-b8ce-0fd4f0e66942)

touched on this in a few places, in a [community meeting](https://hackmd.io/pyhudrC5TgSwdJtpPldHgQ#Agenda-for-July-24th-2024), on a PR I can't find back right now, and in in-person discussions. So here is a new issue with a concrete proposal._

We have experimental support for CuPy, PyTorch and JAX - all of which have CUDA (and in some cases ROCm and/or Intel XPUs) support, which we are not exercising in CI. As a result, we get issues like gh-21486 where someone finds locally that the test suite has regressed for a GPU-enabled array library. This is obviously not ideal, and we'd like a low-overhead and low-cost way to test PRs that a reviewer thinks may impact GPU support in CI.

Scikit-learn has such a CI job, see [the GHA workflow file for it](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/9d39f57399d6f1f7d8e8d4351dbc3e9244b98d28/.github/workflows/cuda-ci.yml) and @betatim's [blog post describing the design](https://betatim.github.io/posts/github-action-with-gpu/). 

The other thing we needed for it was a credit card from NumFOCUS that we can attach to this repo (with a limit on spending level, as described in the blog post) - we have that as well now. So we're all set in principle to add this.

Suggested logistics and design:
- The SciPy core team should sign off on the spending limit (just like we did for Cirrus CI usage). I'd suggest either $50/month or $100/month as the limit, and adjust if needed once we have some experience with it.
- The label-based triggering that scikit-learn has, where adding the label to a PR does a single run and then the label gets removed again so new commits don't auto-run the job again, seems nice - so let's copy it.
- We can start with building SciPy in the GPU-enabled CI runnner; if it becomes expensive we can move the SciPy wheel build to a separate (CPU-only, free) job, upload the wheel to the GHA cache, and then trigger a second job based on the first one to run the CUDA tests.
- The one change from the scikit-learn setup we should make is to use `pixi` rather than `conda-lock`. The latter makes sense for scikit-learn since they were already using it, but `pixi` will work better for us and we've mostly already got the setup worked out in https://github.com/rgommers/pixi-dev-scipystack. I don't want to propose adding that setup in the root of the repo, but only the single environment + lock file that we need to run this GPU job.

#### Reference issue This is a short PR that adds the `jupyterlite_sphinx_strip` tag to the headers for all notebooks under `scipy.stats` first introduced via gh-20303 and gh-21042. This is used to remove unnecessary content from the notebooks when rendering them in JupyterLite (in a new tab, that is). This was first implemented with https://github.com/jupyterlite/jupyterlite-sphinx/releases/tag/v0.16.5 but I realised that I never upstreamed the change here. cc: @melissawm, @rgommers. #### What does this implement/fix? Here's a comparison before v.s. after this PR:
Before

After

#### Additional information When exploring this change with the CircleCI previews, the notebook will fail to open in a new tab, and the file explorer will open instead. To fix this, please add `index.html` before `?path=notebookname.ipynb`. A fix for it upstream is here: https://github.com/jupyterlite/jupyterlite-sphinx/pull/220 (pending merge and release). This is used to remove unnecessary content from the notebooks when rendering them in JupyterLite (in a new tab, that is). This was first implemented with https://github.com/jupyterlite/jupyterlite-sphinx/releases/tag/v0.16.5 but I realised that I never upstreamed the change here. cc: @melissawm, @rgommers. #### What does this implement/fix? Here's a comparison before v.s. after this PR:
Before

After

#### Additional information When exploring this change with the CircleCI previews, the notebook will fail to open in a new tab, and the file explorer will open instead. To fix this, please add `index.html` before `?path=notebookname.ipynb`. A fix for it upstream is here: https://github.com/jupyterlite/jupyterlite-sphinx/pull/220 (pending merge and release). Comment:

As I'm fairly certain the NumPy maintainers are aware of, [conda-forge supports microarch-level builds for x86 archs](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base/#microarch). As NumPy currently doesn't have this enabled I'm going to assume that is for a reason, but to double check and use this as a piece of information for the rest of the community, is that because NumPy already has optimizations that don't require SIMD optimizations to be turned on? Is adding the microarch-level builds going to be too many packages per build for limited utility, and the NumPy team doesn't want to use lots of storage? Are the additional builds enough of a nuisance that this level of maintainer overhead not worthwhile? Have the maintainers not had the opportunity to properly do comparisons on if the level-3 SIMD optimizations are worth it? Something else?

Again, this is asking out of curiosity and to better inform other project decisions, not to insinuate that the NumPy team should be doing something more than everything you already Describe the issue:

The following code seems correct, and passes type checking under 2.1.3 but not 2.2.0:

```python
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(27446968)
n_states = 5

P = rng.random(size=(n_states, n_states))
p = rng.random(n_states)

p = P.T @ p
```

I suppose mypy is flagging that the float width can increase, but since float64 is used by random, that won't happen in this scenario.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
N/A
```


### Error message:

```shell
foo.py:10: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating[Any]]]", variable has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]]")  [assignment]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.13, mypy 1.13, numpy 2.2.0

### Runtime Environment:

I think there are issues with all type checkers. Mypy in addition also removed support for type-checking a regular installed package IIRC. Basically (a) editable installs are fundamentally limited in what they can do/support, and (b) typing tools are going to be limited here as long as they refuse to either run on installed packages in a useful way, or support editable installs with import hooks. The third option is blaming meson-python (and scikit-build-core when used with out-of-tree builds which are also recommended for CMake) for using out-of-tree builds, but that's not all that constructive since it's pretty well established that that's a good way of building with a number of important advantages over littering build artifacts all over the source tree.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/pulls/comments/1888215617","pull_request_review_id":2508368469,"id":1888215617,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABZIq85wi-JB","diff_hunk":"@@ -50,43 +55,87 @@ Other options include: and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, Windows, and Mac. +If you already have Python installed, you can +[install SciPy with `pip`](#pip-install). +For more advanced users who will need to install or upgrade regularly, +you may prefer to use a dedicated package manager like [`uv`] +(see [Installing with `uv`](#uv-install)), +[Conda], or [`pixi`] (see [Installing from conda-forge](#conda-forge-install)). + +[`uv`]: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/ +[Conda]: https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/index.html +[`pixi`]: https://pixi.sh/latest/ + + + +## Installing from PyPI + +SciPy is [available on PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/scipy/), the Python Package Index. + -# Installing with Pip +### Installing with `pip` You can install SciPy from PyPI with `pip`: python -m pip install scipy - {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/pulls/comments/1888222523","pull_request_review_id":2508380486,"id":1888222523,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABZIq85wi_07","diff_hunk":"@@ -3,101 +3,148 @@ title: Installation sidebar: false --- -Installations methods include: +The recommended method of installing SciPy depends on your preferred workflow. +The common workflows can roughly be broken down into the following +categories: -- [Distributions](#distributions) -- [pip](#pip-install) -- [conda](#pip-install) -- [Package Manager](#package_manager) -- [Source](#source) +- [Environment-based (e.g. `pip`, `conda`)](#environment-based) (the traditional workflow) +- [Project-based (e.g. `uv`, `pixi`)](#project-based) (recommended for new users) +- [System package managers](#system-package-managers) (simplest) +- [Building from source](#building-from-source) (for debugging and development) -Methods differ in ease of use, coverage, maintenance of old versions, -system-wide versus local environment use, and control. With pip or -Anaconda\'s conda, you can control the package versions for a specific -project to prevent conflicts. Conda also controls non-Python packages, -like MKL or HDF5. System package managers, like `apt-get`, install -across the entire computer, often have older versions, and don\'t have -as many available versions. Source compilation is much more difficult -but is necessary for debugging and development. If you don\'t know which -installation method you need or prefer, we recommend the Scientific -Python Distribution [Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/). +To install SciPy with [static type stubs], +see [Installing with type stubs](#type-stubs). - +[static type stubs]: https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guides/libraries.html -# Scientific Python Distributions (recommended) + -Python distributions provide the language itself, along with the most -commonly used packages and tools. These downloadable files require -little configuration, work on almost all setups, and provide all the -commonly used scientific Python tools. +## Environment-based workflows -[Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) works on Windows, Mac, -and Linux, provides over 1,500 Python packages, and is used by over 15 -million people. Anaconda is best suited to beginning users; it provides -a large collection of libraries all in one. +In environment-based workflows, you install packages into an environment, which you +can activate and deactivate. -For more advanced users who will need to install or upgrade regularly, -[Mambaforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge#mambaforge) is a more -suitable way to install the `conda` (and `mamba`, a faster `conda` alternative) -package manager. +If you have installed Python, you can create a virtual environment with a tool such +as `venv` - see [the tutorial in the Python Packaging User Guide][venv-guide]. +You can then install [SciPy from PyPI], the Python Package Index, with [`pip`]: -Other options include: + python -m pip install scipy -- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution - including scientific packages and the Spyder IDE; Windows only, but - more actively maintained and supports the latest Python 3 versions. -- [Pyzo](https://pyzo.org): A free distribution based on Anaconda - and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, - Windows, and Mac. +[venv-guide]: https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/tutorials/installing-packages/#creating-virtual-environments +[SciPy from PyPI]: https://pypi.org/project/scipy/ +[`pip`]: https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/getting-started/ - +Alternatively, you can install [SciPy from conda-forge] using a [Conda]-based +environment manager. You can install Python itself, as well as packages in +languages other than Python, as Conda packages. -# Installing with Pip +[Miniforge] is the recommended way to install `conda` and [`mamba`], +two Conda-based environment managers. You can then install SciPy as follows: -You can install SciPy from PyPI with `pip`: + conda install scipy # or + mamba install scipy - python -m pip install scipy +[SciPy from conda-forge]: https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/scipy +[Miniforge]: https://conda-forge.org/download/ +[Conda]: https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/index.html +[`mamba`]: https://mamba.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ + + + +## Project-based workflows + +In project-based workflows, a project is a directory containing a manifest +file describing the project, a lock-file describing the exact dependencies +of the project, and the project's (potentially multiple) environments. +These workflows provide reproducibility benefits over environment-based workflows. + +You can add [SciPy from PyPI] to a [`uv`] project: + + uv add scipy + +You can also install Python itself with `uv`. + +[`uv`]: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/ + +If you work with non-Python packages, you may prefer to install SciPy as +a Conda package, so that you can use the same workflow for packages which +are not available on PyPI. - +You can add [SciPy from conda-forge] to a [`pixi`] project: -# Installing via Conda + pixi add scipy -You can install SciPy from the `defaults` or `conda-forge` channels with `conda`: +Pixi can also install packages from PyPI, using `uv` under the hood. - conda install scipy +[`pixi`]: https://pixi.sh/latest/ - + -# Install system-wide via a package manager +## Installing system-wide via a system package manager System package managers can install the most common Python packages. They install packages for the entire computer, often use older versions, -and don't have as many available versions. +and don't have as many available versions. They are not the recommended +installation method, but they are the simplest. -## Ubuntu and Debian +### Ubuntu and Debian Using `apt-get`: sudo apt-get install python3-scipy -## Fedora +### Fedora Using `dnf`: sudo dnf install python3-scipy -## macOS +### macOS macOS doesn't have a preinstalled package manager, but you can install [Homebrew](https://brew.sh/) and use it to install SciPy (and Python itself): brew install scipy","path":"content/en/install.md","commit_id":"c040bb381df9938ca52c45815e8af46c34eb8ae5","original_commit_id":"c040bb381df9938ca52c45815e8af46c34eb8ae5","user":{"login":"rgommers","id":98330,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjk4MzMw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98330?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers","html_url":"https://github.com/rgommers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"MacPorts is still a thing, but we should keep Homebrew. .github.com/users/leofang/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false}],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-10-28T02:57:31Z","updated_at":"2024-12-17T09:58:44Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"It's been established by the array API standard and DLPack maintainers that users should just use the one-shot function call
```python
arr2 = array_namespace.from_dlpack(arr1)
```
as a (possibly zero-copy) array constructor through the DLPack protocol. Importantly, the capsule should be kept as implementation detail, not exposed to end users. This also allows safer handshaking (correct stream ordering and lifetime management).

The fact that various libraries still keep the legacy, capsule-exposing APIs to me is a maintenance burden, hard to teach, and already causing confusions (ex: https://github.com/dmlc/dlpack/issues/147 is coming from users who have no idea about the new things).

CuPy already marked the old, capsule-consuming API `cupy.fromDlpack()` deprecated, but unfortunately the old, capsule-exposing `cupy.ndarray.toDlpack()` was not yet marked as deprecated. We also have not taught about the new practice in the Interoperability page.

I suggest 
1. we deprecate and remove both in v14 or v15 (I prefer aggressively, but it does not have to be)
2. we update the Interoperability w/ DLPack immediately","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/8703/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/8703/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/comments/2548000161","html_url":"https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/8703#issuecomment-2548000161","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/cupy/cupy/issues/8703","id":2548000161,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBFKgkM6X32Wh","user":{"login":"rgommers","id":98330,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjk4MzMw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98330?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers","html_url":"https://github.com/rgommers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-17T09:58:43Z","updated_at":"2024-12-17T09:58:43Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> I think Jax just added the DLPack 1.0 support recently

Checklist * [x] Used a [personal fork of the feedstock to propose changes](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#forking-and-pull-requests) * [x] Bumped the build number (if the version is unchanged) * [ ] Reset the build number to `0` (if the version changed) * [x] [Re-rendered]( https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/updating_pkgs.html#rerendering-feedstocks ) with the latest `conda-smithy` (Use the phrase @conda-forge-admin, please rerender in a comment in this PR for automated rerendering) * [x] Ensured the license file is being packaged. From the commit message: Upstream keeps all magma-related routines in a separate libtorch_cuda_linalg library that is loaded dynamically whenever linalg functions are used. Given the library is relatively small, splitting it makes it possible to provide "magma" and "nomagma" variants that can be alternated between. Also: Try to speed up magma/nomagma builds a bit. Rather than rebuilding the package 3 times (possibly switching magma → nomagma → magma again), build it twice at the very beginning and store the built files for later reuse in subpackage builds. While at it, replace the `pip wheel` calls with `setup.py build` to avoid unnecessarily zipping up and then unpacking the whole thing. In the end, we are only grabbing a handful of files for `libtorch*` packages and they are in predictable location in the build directory. `pip install` remains being used in the final builds for `pytorch`. Fixes #275 @isuruf helped me a lot with this, particularly with refactoring the builds, so both variants are built in one run.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/pytorch-cpu-feedstock/issues/298/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":1,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/pytorch-cpu-feedstock/issues/298/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/pytorch-cpu-feedstock/issues/comments/2548032583","html_url":"https://github.com/conda-forge/pytorch-cpu-feedstock/pull/298#issuecomment-2548032583","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/conda-forge/pytorch-cpu-feedstock/issues/298","id":2548032583,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCcDMFs6X3-RH","user":{"login":"rgommers","id":98330,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjk4MzMw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98330?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers","html_url":"https://github.com/rgommers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-17T10:12:08Z","updated_at":"2024-12-17T10:12:08Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> FWIW, osx+generic has some failures for 32-bit complex types, where seemingly the entire imaginary part of the result is dropped. Describe your issue.

I have developed a small `pyw` which is using `scipy.fft` internally. Recently, I have noticed that this small script stops running on Windows 11.

As a workaround, I have tried to redirect `stdout` and `stderr` before importing `scipy.fft`:
```py
if sys.executable.endswith('pythonw.exe'):
    sys.stdout = open(os.devnull, 'w')
    sys.stderr = open(os.path.join(os.getenv('TEMP'), 'stderr-' + os.path.basename(sys.argv[0])), 'w')
```
If so, the python script works well but no error message is captured.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import tkinter as tk
from scipy.fft import fft, fftfreq
root = tk.Tk()
root.mainloop()
```


### Error message

```shell
Precisely, there is no error message.
```

ments":1,"created_at":"2024-12-17T04:40:55Z","updated_at":"2024-12-17T11:11:54Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

Fedora has long packaged numpy with f2py as an optional component.  With numpy 1.26.4 without f2py present, you were able to do:
```
from numpy import *
```
without any errors.  Now however:

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
from numpy import *
```


### Error message:

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<python-input-0>", line 1, in <module>
    from numpy import *
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.13/site-packages/numpy/__init__.py", line 361, in __getattr__
    import numpy.f2py as f2py
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'numpy.f2py'
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

2.2.0
3.13.0 (main, Oct  8 2024, 00:00:00) [GCC 14.2.1 20240912 (Red Hat 14.2.1-4)]

### Runtime Environment:

_No response_

### Context for the issue:

See also the discussion in https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2332307

We would like to be able to keep f2py optional as only limited modules make use of it and it brings in a dependency on the python development environment.  Thank you for your consideration.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28016/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28016/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2548175600","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/28016#issuecomment-2548175600","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28016","id":2548175600,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86X4hLw","user":{"login":"rgommers","id":98330,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjk4MzMw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98330?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers","html_url":"https://github.com/rgommers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-17T11:11:52Z","updated_at":"2024-12-17T11:11:52Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Thanks for the report @opoplawski. I relabeled the issue - I wouldn't say it's a bug, since splitting `numpy` into parts like that is not a thing that is supported nor seems very reasonable to do. If any distro does something like that, they're usually responsible for not breaking anything else - and that includes `from numpy import *`. So it seems pretty clear cut that this is a Fedora bug.

That said, let's consider the issue in more detail and see if we're willing to help, or if Fedora should choose between carrying its own patch or undoing the splitting off of `numpy.f2py`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2548175600/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

ments":2,"created_at":"2024-12-17T04:40:55Z","updated_at":"2024-12-17T11:15:08Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

Fedora has long packaged numpy with f2py as an optional component.  With numpy 1.26.4 without f2py present, you were able to do:
```
from numpy import *
```
without any errors.  Now however:

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
from numpy import *
```


### Error message:

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<python-input-0>", line 1, in <module>
    from numpy import *
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.13/site-packages/numpy/__init__.py", line 361, in __getattr__
    import numpy.f2py as f2py
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'numpy.f2py'
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

2.2.0
3.13.0 (main, Oct  8 2024, 00:00:00) [GCC 14.2.1 20240912 (Red Hat 14.2.1-4)]

### Runtime Environment:

_No response_

### Context for the issue:

See also the discussion in https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2332307

We would like to be able to keep f2py optional as only limited modules make use of it and it brings in a dependency on the python development environment.  Thank you for your consideration.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28016/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28016/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2548182288","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/28016#issuecomment-2548182288","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28016","id":2548182288,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86X4i0Q","user":{"login":"rgommers","id":98330,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjk4MzMw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98330?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers","html_url":"https://github.com/rgommers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-17T11:15:06Z","updated_at":"2024-12-17T11:15:06Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"For the record, the reasoning in the upstream bug report seems wrong to me - `f2py` can be used to transpile C code without any other dependencies. Saying that it is unusable without a compiler can also be said of `numpy.distutils`, or of `cython`, etc. - it doesn't seem all that useful to make such a claim.

In general, the correct thing to do for transpilers seems to be to not make them depend on a particular compiler, but leave that to the user of the package - that way they can ensure they are using it with their compiler of choice, rather than forcing one on them.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2548182288/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

 optimize

-Overall this module is in good shape. Two good global optimizers were added in
-1.2.0; large-scale optimizers is still a gap that could be filled.  Other
-things that are needed:
-
-- Many ideas for additional functionality (e.g. integer constraints) in
-  ``linprog``, see `gh-9269 <https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/9269>`__.
-- Add functionality to the benchmark suite to compare results more easily
-  (e.g. with summary plots).
-- deprecate the ``fmin_*`` functions in the documentation, ``minimize`` is
-  preferred.
-- ``scipy.optimize`` has an extensive set of benchmarks for accuracy and speed of
-  the global optimizers. That has allowed adding new optimizers (``shgo`` and
-  ``dual_annealing``) with significantly better performance than the existing
-  ones.  The ``optimize`` benchmark system itself is slow and hard to use
-  however; we need to make it faster and make it easier to compare performance of
-  optimizers via plotting performance profiles.
+It is very challenging to write and maintain optimizers that approach the
+state of the art for large-scale problems. In many cases, it will be more
+appropriate for SciPy to add interfaces to carefully-vetted,

Describe the issue:

The following code seems correct, and passes type checking under 2.1.3 but not 2.2.0:

```python
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(27446968)
n_states = 5

P = rng.random(size=(n_states, n_states))
p = rng.random(n_states)

p = P.T @ p
```

I suppose mypy is flagging that the float width can increase, but since float64 is used by random, that won't happen in this scenario.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
N/A
```


### Error message:

```shell
foo.py:10: error: Incompatible types in assignment (expression has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating[Any]]]", variable has type "ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]]")  [assignment]
Found 1 error in 1 file (checked 1 source file)
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.13, mypy 1.13, numpy 2.2.0

Brief description of what is fixed or changed

Hi all! This PR adds a "Translations team" section to the teams page. The toml file is manually added since translators are members of crowdin but not necessarily github.

I am happy to add links to github profiles and/or new avatars, just let me know. As a placeholder, folks without an avatar are showing up as the NumPy logo.

Closes #796

+````````````````````````","path":"doc/source/dev/roadmap-detailed.rst","commit_id":"2c225634b92d1ee154a2d1ccbad6b1eefe97cf55","original_commit_id":"c300d1b3671a27310520105619b60d81d6512b63","user":{"login":"rgommers","id":98330,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjk4MzMw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98330?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers","html_url":"https://github.com/rgommers","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rgommers/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Here is how I'd rewrite this section:
```
SciPy owes a lot of its success to relying on wrapping well established
Fortran libraries (QUADPACK, FITPACK, ODRPACK, ODEPACK etc). The Fortran 77
that these libraries are written in is quite hard to maintain, and the use
of Fortran is problematic for many reasons, e.g., it makes our wheel builds
much harder to maintain, it has repeatedly been problematic for supporting
new platforms like macOS arm64 and Windows on Arm, and it is highly problematic
for Pyodide's SciPy support. Our goal is to remove all Fortran code from SciPy,
by replacing the functionality with code written in other languages. Progress
towards this goal is tracked in `gh-18566 <https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/18566>`__.
```","created_at":"2024-12-18T07:28:44Z","updated_at":"2024-12-18T07:28:44Z","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22101#discussion_r1889745946","pull_request_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/pulls/22101","author_association":"MEMBER","_links":{"self":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/pulls/comments/1889745946"},"html":{"href":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22101#discussion_r1889745946"},"pull_request":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/pulls/22101"}},"reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/pulls/comments/1889745946/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"start_line":null,"original_start_line":null,"start_side":null,"line":82,"original_line":82,"side":"RIGHT","in_reply_to_id":1888325241,"original_position":7,"position":7,"subject_type":"line"},"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/pulls/22101","id":2238597863,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABZIoc6Fbkrn","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22101","diff_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22101.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/22101.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/22101","number":22101,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"DOC: Describe your issue.

We are facing performance issue while running python 3.11 on jupyterhub 4 with scipy version 1.14.1 . But performance improves drastically when we are using scipy version 1.12.0

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX
from scipy import stats
import multiprocessing
import time

# Function to simulate SARIMA fitting and use scipy for statistical test
def sarima_fit_and_test(data, order, seasonal_order):
    # Fit SARIMA model
    model = SARIMAX(data, order=order, seasonal_order=seasonal_order)
    results = model.fit(disp=False)
    
    # Perform a basic statistical test using scipy (e.g., Jarque-Bera test for normality)
    jb_stat, jb_p_value = stats.jarque_bera(results.resid)
    
    # Return the results and p-value of the test
    return results.aic, jb_p_value

# Function to parallelize SARIMA fitting using multiprocessing
def run_parallel_sarima(data, orders, seasonal_orders):
    # Creating a pool of workers
    with multiprocessing.Pool(processes=multiprocessing.cpu_count()) as pool:
        # Prepare tasks for parallel execution
        tasks = [(data, orders[i], seasonal_orders[i]) for i in range(len(orders))]
        
        # Run SARIMA fitting in parallel
        results = pool.starmap(sarima_fit_and_test, tasks)
    
    return results

# Main function to generate synthetic data and run the performance test
def performance_test():
    # Generate synthetic data (e.g., seasonal time series)
    np.random.seed(42)
    n = 200
    trend = np.linspace(10, 50, n)
    seasonal = 10 * np.sin(np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, n))
    noise = np.random.normal(0, 1, n)
    data = pd.Series(trend + seasonal + noise)
    
    # Define SARIMA orders to test
    orders = [(1, 1, 1), (2, 1, 2), (1, 1, 2), (2, 1, 1)]
    seasonal_orders = [(1, 1, 1, 12), (1, 1, 2, 12), (2, 1, 1, 12), (1, 0, 1, 12)]
    
    # Record start time for performance testing
    start_time = time.time()
    
    # Run the performance test in parallel
    results = run_parallel_sarima(data, orders, seasonal_orders)
    
    # Output results and total execution time
    for idx, (aic, p_value) in enumerate(results):
        print(f"Model {idx + 1}: AIC = {aic:.4f}, Jarque-Bera p-value = {p_value:.4f}")
    
    print(f"Performance Test Completed in {time.time() - start_time:.2f} seconds")

# Run the performance test
if __name__ == "__main__":
    performance_test()

```


### Error message

```shell
No errors message only slow performance
```


### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
Scipy=1.14.1
Python =3.11.7
```

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
in Python 3.13 directory:
./configure --enable-optimizations --enable-experimental-jit --disable-gil 
make 
make test
make install
python3 -m venv myenv
source myenv/bin/activate
pip install numpy (installation ok)
pip install scipy (errors)

When configuring without "--disable-gil" everything is ok
```
